votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the result matches the boolean value of the disjunction of the three equality conditions.
The Python code correctly implements the logical equivalence defined in the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the boolean result matches the logical disjunction of the three equality conditions.

The Coq spec defines result = true iff one of the three sum conditions holds. The Python code returns True iff result equals the boolean value of those same conditions. For concrete inputs, when result is a boolean, both agree: if any condition holds, Coq requires result=true and Python checks result==True; if none hold, Coq requires result=false and Python checks result==False. The logical equivalence matches.

The Coq spec defines result = true iff one of the three sum conditions holds. The Python code returns True iff result equals the boolean value of those same conditions. For concrete inputs, when result is a boolean, both agree: if any condition holds, Coq requires result=true and Python checks result==True; if none hold, Coq requires result=false and Python checks result==False. The logical equivalence matches.

The Coq spec defines result = true iff one of the three sum conditions holds. The Python code returns True iff result equals the boolean value of the same disjunction. For concrete inputs, when result is a boolean, both agree: if result is True, Python checks the disjunction is True; if result is False, Python checks the disjunction is False. This matches the Coq biconditional semantics.
Python returns True iff result equals the truth of (x=y+z or y=x+z or z=y+x), matching the Coq iff between result=true and that condition, with matching integer semantics.
Both interpret integers as unbounded and check that result equals whether x=y+z or y=x+z or z=y+x; the Python function returns True exactly when the Coq bidirectional spec holds.
Python returns True exactly when result equals whether any of x=y+z, y=x+z, or z=x+y holds, matching the Coq iff specification over integers.
